description: Vitamin D food supplement with health claims: supplement directive, claims regulation, fortification, labelling

pipeline_inputs.query_text: vitamin D food supplement permitted health claim labelling dosage requirements

ground_truth_requirements.description: Only vitamins and minerals listed in Annex I in the forms listed in Annex II may be used in the manufacture of food supplements
ground_truth_requirements.notes: Art 3 has scope/definitions; Art 4 has the substance list requirement. May match either article.

ground_truth_requirements.description: Food supplement labelling must include: 'food supplement' designation, names and amounts of vitamins/minerals, recommended daily portion, warning not to exceed stated dose, statement that supplements should not replace a varied diet, storage out of reach of children
Maximum amounts of vitamins and minerals in food supplements shall be set considering upper safe intake levels and intake from other dietary sources
Nutrition and health claims must not be false, ambiguous, or misleading, and must be substantiated by generally accepted scientific evidence
Health claims are only permitted if the nutrient is present in a significant amount as defined by EU legislation and the substance can be used by the body
Health claims are only permitted if authorised and included in the Union list of permitted claims, and must be accompanied by specific mandatory statements
Vitamins and minerals may only be added to foods from the sources listed in Annex II and in bioavailable forms
Food business operators must establish traceability at all stages of production, processing and distribution
Food supplement labelling, presentation and advertising must not attribute to supplements the property of preventing, treating or curing human disease
The amount of each nutrient or substance with a nutritional or physiological effect must be expressed as a percentage of reference values on the label
When a nutrition or health claim is made on a food supplement, nutrition information must be provided in accordance with FIC regulation
Foods with added vitamins and minerals must not claim that a balanced diet cannot provide adequate amounts, and labelling must not mislead consumers
When adding a vitamin or mineral to food, the total amount must be at least 15% of the reference intake per 100g/100ml/per package (significant amount)
Health claims on foods may only be made if they appear in the authorised list set out in the Annex to this regulation
Food must not be placed on the market if it is unsafe (injurious to health or unfit for human consumption)
When a nutrition or health claim is made, nutrition labelling is mandatory and must include the amount of the substance to which the claim refers
